topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The string quartet was playing a saccharine waltz, and I was trying desperately to look engaged in the artisanal cheese platter. Then, across the crowded ballroom, a familiar silhouette materialized. It was him. My ex. He was laughing, his arm draped casually around a woman whose smile seemed too bright, too effortless. My breath hitched, a sharp, shallow intake that felt like swallowing broken glass. My hands, resting on a lukewarm glass of champagne, suddenly felt clammy, the crystal slick beneath my anxious grip. I wanted to disappear. I started to pivot, a jerky, unplanned movement, hoping the crowd would swallow me whole, but I only managed to bump into a waiter carrying a tray of canapés, sending a small cascade of tiny appetizers tumbling to the marble floor. Every nerve ending in my body was screaming, a high-pitched whine beneath the music. I mumbled something incoherent to the waiter, my voice sounding thin and reedy, and fled toward the relative sanctuary of the terrace, needing air that didn't feel suffocating.

[story 2]

I had rehearsed my strategy for the evening: observe, nod politely, and maintain a respectable distance. The reality, however, struck me like a physical blow when I spotted him near the entrance, sharing a knowing glance with someone I vaguely recognized. My carefully constructed composure fractured instantly. A sudden, intense heat bloomed behind my eyes, making the bright lights of the reception feel blindingly harsh. I felt a strange pressure building in my chest, like a trapped bird beating its wings against my ribs. I stopped mid-conversation with my friend, my mouth suddenly dry and tight. I watched as he turned, and for a split second, time seemed to warp. My vision tunneled. I gripped the edge of the linen tablecloth so hard my knuckles turned white, and a desperate need to escape the suffocating proximity of the scene overwhelmed me. I excused myself abruptly, the sound of my own hurried footsteps echoing loudly in the sudden silence of my retreat.

[story 3]

The music swelled, a wave of joyous noise washing over the room, but all I could focus on was the man standing near the punch bowl. He looked exactly the same, yet utterly foreign, and a jolt of sheer panic shot straight through my core. My stomach performed a violent flip, churning with an unwelcome, frantic energy. I tried to walk past, to find an exit, but my legs felt disconnected from my will. A tremor started in my hands, making it impossible to hold my wine glass steady. Every sound seemed amplified—the clinking glasses, the booming laughter—and it all coalesced into a deafening static in my ears. I found myself staring, rooted to the spot, unable to move forward or backward, trapped in a tight coil of sheer, overwhelming internal turmoil. I squeezed my eyes shut for a brief second, fighting the urge to bolt, and then, with a sudden, jerky motion, I spun around and practically fled the vicinity, needing absolute solitude to reassemble myself.
